finding: Plan omits the preload/renderer/IPC dead-code blast radius. Removing the migration path from main/sidecar.ts and main/index.ts leaves orphan code across 4 files: (1) preload/types.ts — SqliteMigrationProgress type and the onSqliteMigrationProgress ElectronAPI method become unreachable; (2) preload/index.ts — the sqlite-migration-progress IPC listener is registered but never fires; (3) renderer/loading.tsx — imports SqliteMigrationProgress, subscribes to a channel that emits nobody; its 'Migrating your database' text and progress bar become dead UI; (4) main/ipc.ts — sendSqliteMigrationProgress export becomes dead code when its only caller (sendSqliteMigrationProgress calls in index.ts) is removed. These don't block boot but the plan should either include them in the cleanup or explicitly document them as intentionally deferred and record a coordinator seam.
repair_path: Add a deferred-seam section to the plan listing the 4 orphan files. For each file, state whether it remains untouched (deferred cleanup) or gets cleaned up in this slice. If deferred, open a follow-on slice to remove SqliteMigrationProgress type, onSqliteMigrationProgress API method, the IPC channel, and the loading-window migration text. The loading.tsx status line 'Migrating your database' will become a lie if sqlite_waiting is never emitted — that is a visible defect, not just dead code.

finding: Plan's server.ts change set is incomplete. Removing onSqliteProgress from SpawnLocalServerOptions (line 29 in server.ts) without also removing the sqlite message handler block (lines 121-125) creates a type error — options.onSqliteProgress?.(message.progress) references a property that no longer exists on the options type. If both are removed, the SidecarMessage union type (lines 14-18) still carries the sqlite variant as a dead member, and the SqliteMigrationProgress import (line 8) becomes unused. The postMessage call (lines 149-156) passes needsMigration but the plan says to remove it from options — the call site must also stop passing it. The plan should enumerate every line-level change in server.ts, not a vague 'simplify spawn function'.
repair_path: Specify the exact change set for server.ts: (1) Remove needsMigration and onSqliteProgress from SpawnLocalServerOptions; (2) Remove the entire message.type === 'sqlite' handler block (lines 121-125); (3) Remove SqliteMigrationProgress import (line 8); (4) Remove SqliteMigrationProgress from SidecarMessage union (line 15) or keep the union variant but annotate as unused; (5) Remove needsMigration from child.postMessage call (line 155). Verify type checking passes after.

finding: Plan understates the needsMigration footprint in index.ts. Three sites reference needsMigration, not two: (1) the computed constant at line 278-284; (2) the sqlite progress event emitter at lines 317-321; (3) the conditional loading-window creation at lines 356-367. The plan names sites (1) and (2) but not (3). The line 356 branch ('if (needsMigration)') creates a loading window that would show migration progress — without needsMigration this branch is dead but still compiles. The related import of sendSqliteMigrationProgress (line 17) becomes unused. The plan should explicitly include removing the line 356-367 block and removing sendSqliteMigrationProgress from the import, or explain why the loading-window logic stays.
repair_path: Add to the index.ts change list: remove lines 356-367 (the 'if (needsMigration)' loading window block) — this block can also be removed because without the migration event, the initEmitter.on('sqlite') listener is also gone, so the loading window would never show useful progress. Remove sendSqliteMigrationProgress from the import on line 17. Remove SqliteMigrationProgress from the type import on line 14 if it's otherwise unused after cleanup.

finding: No data-loss risk assessment for the removed migration path. The needsMigration check in index.ts (line 278-284) detects absence of a SQLite DB file and was used to trigger JSON→SQLite migration. The plan asserts this was a "one-time JSON→SQLite migration" but doesn't answer: what happens to users who (a) have a JSON data directory from a pre-migration version and never upgraded through the SQLite migration? (b) have an existing SQLite DB that was never migrated to Postgres? The server now calls applyMigrations() on Postgres — does that handle the empty case, or does the user lose access to existing JSON data? The plan should either (1) document that applyMigrations() handles both the fresh and data-upgrade paths, or (2) add a data-preservation check that warns the user if legacy data is detected without a migration path.
repair_path: Add a safety section to the plan that: (1) confirms the applyMigrations() signature — does it accept an existing data directory or only handle fresh schema bootstrap? (2) documents whether any legacy data paths (JSON files, existing SQLite) are readable by the Postgres server; (3) if data loss is possible, adds a pre-boot check in index.ts that detects old data directories and either migrates or warns. At minimum, the plan must state the assessed risk level explicitly rather than leaving it implicit.

finding: env.d.ts is a fragile hand-maintained type barrier that will diverge again. The current file mirrors virtual:opencode-server exports by hand — it already namespaces exports (Server, Config, Log, Database, JsonMigration) that the actual source (node.ts) has moved away from. node.ts uses export * as Log from "@opencode-ai/core/util/log" (namespace projection) but exports Config and Server as plain named exports. The env.d.ts uses export namespace wrappers that duplicate and potentially conflict with the real type structure. JsonMigration is the third stale entry (after bootstrap was already folded). This pattern guarantees future breakage every time node.ts changes its export surface — exactly the failure mode causing this incident.
repair_path: Three long-run recommendations: (1) Generate env.d.ts from the actual node.ts exports via a small build-time script — the electron-vite config already resolves virtual:opencode-server to node.js, so derive the type declaration from source; (2) Eliminate the manual namespace wrappers in env.d.ts and use a single re-export: 'export * from "../../../opencode/dist/types/src/node"' with only necessary overrides; (3) Add a CI type-level contract test that imports virtual:opencode-server and asserts the expected export shape, catching divergence before the next boot failure.
